content.text: The two tools at the top of the Python async expert tier,
is a drop-in performance accelerant for
built on top of the same C library that powers Node.js.
is an abstraction layer that lets code run on top of multiple async backends —

, or anything else — without being tied to one. Together they represent the two most important expert-level topics in Python async: squeezing more performance out of the existing event loop, and writing async code that survives a changing ecosystem.
is a replacement for the default Python
event loop. It is written in Cython and uses

— the same asynchronous I/O library that underpins Node.js — as its C-level engine. Once installed, a single line of code replaces Python's pure-Python event loop with a C-accelerated one that speaks the same
, FastAPI, virtually any async library — continues to work without modification. The application code does not change at all.
Why it matters and what it solves

event loop is implemented in Python, with a small amount of C for critical paths. That design makes it readable and portable, but the event loop itself introduces overhead on every callback dispatch, file descriptor poll, and timer check. At scale, that overhead compounds.
replaces the I/O polling, the callback scheduler, the timer heap, and the socket machinery with
, which is aggressively optimized battle-tested C code that has been tuned for years. The result is that the event loop itself becomes nearly zero-overhead compared to the actual I/O operations.

Benchmarks consistently show uvloop running web frameworks and database clients at 2× to 4× the throughput of the standard event loop for I/O-heavy workloads. It makes Python async I/O servers competitive with Go and Node.js on raw throughput benchmarks.
(what to run, when to run it, task scheduling) from the
(how to watch sockets, how to wait for time to pass). The policy lives in
's Python classes. The implementation — the event loop — is pluggable via the
provides a new event loop implementation that fulfils the same interface but replaces all the Python-level polling machinery with calls into
's epoll/kqueue/IOCP layer. Everything else — task scheduling,
— is still handled by Python's asyncio, because those parts are pure scheduling logic, not I/O.
The speed comes from two sources: (1)

makes fewer system calls than the Python event loop's select/epoll wrapping, and (2) the inner hot loop — the code that runs after every single I/O event to dispatch the next callback — executes in compiled C rather than interpreted Python bytecode.

The core benefit is throughput. More requests per second from the same CPU and the same application code. Secondary benefits include lower per-request latency at high concurrency (fewer Python bytecodes executed per I/O event), and better tail latency because
's timer resolution is tighter than the Python selector.
Memory usage typically improves slightly as well because
uses more efficient internal data structures than Python's selector loop.
only works on Unix (Linux, macOS). Windows is not supported because
on Windows uses IOCP, which has a different model, and the
Cython wrapper was not written to support it. For Windows development or deployment, the standard event loop must be used.

is also a compiled C extension. It requires compilation at install time (or a binary wheel matching the Python version), which adds friction to deployments using Alpine Linux or other distroless environments where binary wheel support may be limited.
reimplements the event loop in C, debugging internal event loop behavior becomes harder — the C-level stack frames are not Python frames, so tools like
's debug mode and some profilers lose visibility into certain paths.
does not help with CPU-bound work. If the bottleneck is compute rather than I/O, replacing the event loop does nothing.

when running an I/O-heavy Python async application on Linux or macOS and throughput is a bottleneck. The typical cases are: HTTP servers (aiohttp, FastAPI/uvicorn, Starlette), async database clients (asyncpg), and message queue consumers. Essentially, any async Python server that needs to maximize requests per second is a candidate.

Do not use it in the following situations: Windows-only deployments, environments where binary wheels cannot be installed (some minimal Docker images), code that uses asyncio internals or event loop private APIs that uvloop does not implement, or anywhere that the bottleneck is CPU rather than I/O.
There are three ways to activate it, from simplest to most explicit:

import uvloop
uvloop.install()  # replaces the default event loop policy globally

# Now any asyncio.run() or asyncio.get_event_loop() uses uvloop
import asyncio

async def main():
    await asyncio.sleep(1)

asyncio.run(main())

import uvloop

async def main():
    ...

uvloop.run(main())  # equivalent to asyncio.run() but forces uvloop
Option 3 — Set the event loop policy manually

import asyncio
import uvloop

asyncio.set_event_loop_policy(uvloop.EventLoopPolicy())

async def main():
    ...

asyncio.run(main())
is the most common approach for applications.
is useful for scripts where you want to be explicit. The policy approach is useful when configuring the loop in framework setup code.
For a FastAPI application served by Uvicorn, uvloop is already used automatically when it is installed — Uvicorn detects and activates it. No code changes are needed.
-backed API server handling 10,000 database-connected requests per second on a single core benefits dramatically from
. Without it, every I/O event — each database read, each HTTP response write — passes through Python's selector machinery. With
's C callback system, and the difference is measurable: published benchmarks for asyncpg with uvloop show it processing PostgreSQL queries at comparable throughput to the Go
In practice, replacing the event loop in a production FastAPI service with a
call has been observed to reduce average response latency by 15–40% in I/O-heavy endpoints, purely from the event loop overhead reduction.
The most common mistake is installing
is never called. A simple check is to assert
A second mistake is assuming uvloop helps with CPU-bound tasks. If a coroutine runs a heavy computation without yielding, uvloop does nothing — the GIL is still held, and no other coroutine can run.
Third: using uvloop on Windows in CI but trying to deploy on Linux. The install fails on Windows, breaking the CI pipeline. The fix is to make the activation conditional:

import sys
if sys.platform != 'win32':
    import uvloop
    uvloop.install()
is a compatibility layer — a "structured concurrency" library — that sits between application code and async backends. Code written against the

, or any other backend that provides an anyio adapter. It also implements its own structured concurrency primitives (task groups, cancel scopes) that are significantly safer and more composable than raw
is the engine inside Starlette and FastAPI's concurrency machinery. When FastAPI runs background tasks or anyio-native libraries,
The Python async ecosystem split early between
(an alternative with a strict structured concurrency model). The split meant that a library written for one backend could not be used in a project using the other.

solves this by providing a unified API covering the most common async operations — task spawning, cancel scopes, streams, synchronization, and file/socket I/O — with implementations that compile down to whichever backend is running. A library author writes against
once, and users can use it with either
, where they are far safer than raw task management.
Before going further, the mental model for structured concurrency needs to be clear.

, a task can be created anywhere, at any time, and it outlives the scope that created it. This creates invisible lifetimes: a task spawned inside a function can keep running after the function returns, holding resources, raising exceptions that go unhandled, and preventing clean shutdown. This is the same problem as detached threads.
child tasks cannot outlive the scope that spawned them
. When the scope exits — normally or via exception — all child tasks are cancelled and awaited before the scope returns. This makes the lifetime of concurrent work as predictable as a function call.
A task group is a context manager that owns a set of concurrent tasks. When the
block exits, it waits for all tasks to finish. If any task raises an exception, all other tasks in the group are cancelled, and the exception propagates to the caller as an

import anyio

async def fetch(url: str) -> None:
    await anyio.sleep(0.1)  # simulate I/O
    print(f"done: {url}")

async def main():
    async with anyio.create_task_group() as tg:
        tg.start_soon(fetch, "https://api.example.com/a")
        tg.start_soon(fetch, "https://api.example.com/b")
        tg.start_soon(fetch, "https://api.example.com/c")
    # All three tasks have completed (or all were cancelled) by the time we reach here
    print("all done")

anyio.run(main)
The task group guarantees that no task spawned inside it can outlive the
block. This is structurally different from
, which has subtle edge cases around exception propagation and cancellation.

A cancel scope is a context manager that limits the lifetime or duration of the code running inside it. Cancelling a scope cancels all tasks and operations within it. A deadline can be set to auto-cancel after a timeout.

import anyio

async def main():
    with anyio.move_on_after(5.0) as scope:
        await anyio.sleep(10.0)       # Will be cancelled after 5 seconds
    
    if scope.cancelled_caught:
        print("The operation timed out — but we're still running normally")
    
    # Alternatively, raise on timeout:
    with anyio.fail_after(5.0):
        await anyio.sleep(10.0)       # Raises TimeoutError after 5 seconds
silently exits the scope on timeout (the code after the
. Cancel scopes can be nested, and inner scopes can catch cancellations meant for outer scopes — giving fine-grained control over which parts of a concurrent computation are given up on first.

# asyncio — raw task cancellation is fragile
import asyncio

async def risky():
    task = asyncio.create_task(some_coro())
    try:
        await asyncio.wait_for(task, timeout=5.0)
    except asyncio.TimeoutError:
        task.cancel()
        try:
            await task
        except asyncio.CancelledError:
            pass  # silently absorbed — easy to miss exceptions

# anyio — cancel scopes make this safe and readable
import anyio

async def safe():
    with anyio.fail_after(5.0):
        await some_coro()
    # Timeout automatically cleaned up, exceptions not silently absorbed

import anyio

async def main():
    print("running on", anyio.get_current_task())

anyio.run(main)                        # defaults to asyncio
anyio.run(main, backend="asyncio")     # explicit asyncio
anyio.run(main, backend="trio")        # requires pip install trio
When writing a library intended for others to use, using
can integrate the library without friction. This is why
-native streaming libraries, and FastAPI internals all use
provides its own synchronization primitives that work on both backends:

import anyio

async def producer(send_channel):
    async with send_channel:
        for i in range(5):
            await send_channel.send(i)

async def consumer(receive_channel):
    async with receive_channel:
        async for item in receive_channel:
            print(f"received: {item}")

async def main():
    send_channel, receive_channel = anyio.create_memory_object_stream(max_buffer_size=10)
    async with anyio.create_task_group() as tg:
        tg.start_soon(producer, send_channel)
        tg.start_soon(consumer, receive_channel)

anyio.run(main)
creates a typed, buffered channel — cleaner than
for producer/consumer patterns and portable across backends.
— all with the same backend-agnostic interface.
provides async wrappers for file and network operations:

import anyio

async def read_file():
    async with await anyio.open_file("data.txt") as f:
        content = await f.read()
    return content

async def tcp_echo_server():
    listener = await anyio.create_tcp_listener(local_port=8080)
    async with listener:
        await listener.serve(handle_client)

async def handle_client(client):
    async with client:
        data = await client.receive(1024)
        await client.send(data)
/ native asyncio sockets behind a backend-agnostic interface.
is compatible with code that uses raw
-run coroutine on the asyncio backend, all standard
modules allow bridging sync and async code:

import anyio

def blocking_computation(x):
    # Runs in a thread pool, doesn't block the event loop
    return x * x

async def main():
    result = await anyio.to_thread.run_sync(blocking_computation, 42)
    print(result)  # 1764

anyio.run(main)
, but it works on both asyncio and trio, and it respects
— so it can enforce a global cap on how many blocking threads run concurrently.
Architecture: how anyio dispatches to backends
is called, anyio selects its asyncio backend implementation and delegates all primitive operations through it. On
, the same dispatch goes to trio's nurseries and cancel scopes. The application code never calls
, and anyio translates that to whatever the current backend provides.
when writing a library that will be consumed by others who might use any async backend. The
approach is also recommended for new application code because its task group and cancel scope model is safer than raw

structured concurrency (even on asyncio-only codebases) when correctness under cancellation matters — auth servers, request pipelines, anything where a partially-cancelled operation could leave state corrupt. The structured model makes these safe by default.

when the codebase is already asyncio-only, has no plans to port to another backend, and the team is comfortable with raw asyncio. In that case, anyio adds a dependency and a learning curve with limited payoff.
Tasks created this way are not tracked by the task group and can leak. Inside anyio code, always use
On the asyncio backend these happen to work, but they interact badly — an asyncio timeout can cancel a task that anyio's cancel scope believes it still owns. Use
When multiple tasks in a task group raise exceptions, anyio raises an
is a top-level entry point. Inside an already-running async context (like a Jupyter notebook or a FastAPI endpoint), use the existing event loop and call async functions with
sits above the event loop abstraction. Activating

import uvloop
import anyio

uvloop.install()  # Install uvloop as the asyncio event loop

async def main():
    async with anyio.create_task_group() as tg:
        tg.start_soon(work_a)
        tg.start_soon(work_b)

anyio.run(main, backend="asyncio")
# anyio dispatches to asyncio, which now uses uvloop under the hood
This combination is the recommended production setup for high-throughput Python async servers on Linux:
provides safe structured concurrency, backend portability, and clean cancellation
provides the task and synchronization machinery
behind a platform check so Windows environments do not break. Activate it as early as possible in the process — ideally before any event loops are created. Use
in servers. Do not use uvloop in test code that patches the event loop, as it can interfere with mocking.
: write libraries against anyio primitives rather than raw asyncio. Use
to bound thread pool usage when calling
for operations where timeout is expected behavior (not an error). Always handle
when multiple concurrent tasks may fail. Use
when crossing the thread/async boundary from synchronous code.
and published as binary wheels for CPython on Linux and macOS. It ships pre-integrated with
is maintained by the same author as
(Alex Grönholm). It is the internal concurrency engine for
, Starlette, FastAPI, and several async database connectors.
is available separately; installing it alongside
) enables test functions to run on multiple backends via parameterization:

import pytest
import anyio

@pytest.mark.anyio
async def test_something():
    async with anyio.create_task_group() as tg:
        tg.start_soon(anyio.sleep, 0)
This runs the test twice — once under each backend — guaranteeing portability.
(Python 3.11+) gives asyncio a structured concurrency primitive, but without cancel scopes, backend portability, or the thread bridging utilities that

provides. For greenfield projects on Python 3.11+, the gap between raw asyncio and anyio has narrowed for simple use cases, but anyio remains the better choice for library authors and for code that needs nested cancellation.
replaces the asyncio event loop with a C/libuv implementation, typically delivering 2–4× throughput improvement for I/O-heavy workloads with zero application code changes, but only on Unix.
is a structured concurrency layer that makes async code portable across
backends, and brings safe task group / cancel scope semantics to asyncio even before Python 3.11.
Task groups enforce that no child task outlives its parent scope — this is the structural concurrency guarantee that prevents the "fire and forget" leaks common in raw asyncio.
patterns with composable, nestable timeout regions.
The two tools compose without conflict: activate
on the asyncio backend to get both performance and safe concurrency.
, not raw asyncio, to remain usable in any async ecosystem their users prefer.
enables automatic multi-backend testing to verify backend-agnostic behavior.
